nativeRange.habitat: Protected upper reef slopes and fore-reef slopes in clear, tropical waters; commonly collected from the Great Barrier Reef and Indo-Pacific reefs

decorPreferences: open area with spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding of small meaty foods — mysis shrimp, chopped krill, brine shrimp, or LPS pellet/powder mixes — is recommended once per week. Feed during evening hours when tentacles are extended; turning off flow during feeding improves uptake. Overfeeding has been reported to cause tissue deformation.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer without spacing
bulldozing organisms such as large hermit crabs or urchins that may damage tissue

summary: Lobophyllia is a genus of fleshy LPS brain corals prized for vivid color and hardiness. Generally considered intermediate care; tolerates moderate lighting and low-moderate flow with weekly target feeding.
careNotes:
Lobophyllia (commonly called lobo coral or lobed brain coral) is a genus of Large Polyp Stony (LPS) corals in the family Lobophylliidae (Order Scleractinia). The genus was previously placed in Mussidae by some sources; Lobophylliidae is the current accepted family. The scientificName is set to 'Lobophyllia sp.' to reflect that multiple species (including L. hemprichii, L. corymbosa, L. hataii, L. robusta) are traded interchangeably in the hobby with near-identical husbandry requirements.

Note on trade name overlap: The trade name 'Lord coral' and 'Aussie Lord' typically refer to Acanthastrea lordhowensis (now reclassified as Micromussa lordhowensis), which is a separate genus. Lobophyllia and Acanthastrea/Micromussa are sometimes confused in trade but are distinct genera with similar care requirements. Specimens labeled 'Lord coral' from Australian sources should be verified to species.

Lighting: Generally advised to place in moderate light (approximately 75–150 PAR). Sources vary on the recommended range — some hobbyist guides suggest 50–100 PAR to prevent bleaching, while others cite 150–200 PAR as acceptable once acclimated. New specimens are best introduced at the lower end and moved incrementally. Avoid intense SPS-level lighting.

Flow: Low to moderate water movement is generally recommended. Direct laminar flow tends to cause tissue retraction and potential recession; indirect, randomized flow at roughly 5–15× tank volume per hour tends to work well. Flow should be sufficient to clear detritus from the coral's surface without stressing it.

Placement: Best positioned on the sandbed or lowest rockwork. Allow a minimum of 10–15 cm (4–6 inches) of clear space around the colony on all sides. Lobophyllia tends to extend mesenteric filaments nocturnally to attack neighboring corals — these filaments retract by morning and may go unnoticed. Physical sweeper tentacles have been reported to reach up to 12 cm (approximately 5 inches) in some specimens.

Feeding: Lobophyllia has been known to benefit meaningfully from regular target feeding. Weekly feedings of mysis shrimp, chopped krill, brine shrimp, or commercial LPS foods are commonly recommended. Some specialist sources advise reducing frequency to once every two weeks to avoid tissue distension from overfeeding; weekly is a reasonable middle ground. Feeding is best done in the evening when tentacles are extended, with flow pumps off for 15–30 minutes.

Care level disagreement: Sources range from 'easy/beginner' (LiveAquaria, TFH Magazine) to 'intermediate' (Tidal Gardens, MetaReef search consensus) to 'advanced' (ReefChasers). The 'advanced' rating from ReefChasers appears to reflect their specific colony selection and fragmentation difficulty rather than baseline husbandry. 'Intermediate' is adopted as the averaged consensus — Lobophyllia is generally more forgiving than SPS corals but requires stable water quality and thoughtful placement.

Water quality: Stable parameters are more important than hitting exact targets. Elevated nitrate (above 30–40 ppm) and phosphate cause tissue stress; very low-nutrient conditions can also stress the coral. Maintain moderate nutrients (nitrate 5–15 ppm, phosphate 0.05–0.1 ppm) for best coloration and growth. Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation (two-part or reactor) is recommended for sustained skeletal growth.

Growth: Lobophyllia tend to be slow growers in captivity. Wild colonies can extend several meters across; aquarium colonies typically reach 20–40 cm over years of growth.

Fragmentation: Lobophyllia can be fragged but recovery is slow and risk of tissue necrosis is higher than with faster-growing LPS. Not routinely fragged in the hobby.
breedingNotes: Lobophyllia reproduces sexually via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. Captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby. Fragmentation (cutting) is technically possible but Lobophyllia are slow-healing and higher-risk to frag than many other LPS corals; it is not commonly attempted.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source. Listed scientific name Lobophyllia hemprichii; care level 'Easy'; temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C); SG 1.023–1.025; pH 8.1–8.4; dKH 8–12; lighting moderate-to-high; flow medium; placement all areas; family listed as Mussidae (now superseded by Lobophylliidae).

additional.name: ReefChasers – Lobophyllia Care Guide
additional.notes: Secondary source. PAR 150–200 (moderate); temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C); pH 8.0–8.4; dKH 8–9; Ca 400–450 ppm; care level 'Advanced'; flow moderate; placement sandbed/lower; sweeper tentacle reach up to 5 inches (~12.7 cm); feeding 2–3× weekly mysis/brine. Care level averaged to 'intermediate' across all sources.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Lobophyllia Coral Care
additional.notes: Secondary source. PAR 30–150; dKH 8–9; Ca ~425 ppm; Mg ~1350 ppm; flow low-moderate; placement lower/sandbed; care level intermediate; native range Pacific/Great Barrier Reef. Confirmed nocturnal mesenteric filament aggression.

additional.name: TFH Magazine – Lobophyllia hemprichii
additional.notes: Cross-verification source (TFH is a whitelist secondary for coral-saltwater). Temperature 75–83°F (24–28°C); SG 1.024–1.026; pH 8.1–8.2; Ca 380–430 ppm; Mg ~1272 ppm; dKH 3.5 meq/l (~9.8 dKH); care level beginner/easy; native range Indo-West Pacific and Red Sea including Australia east to Tahiti, north to Japan. Feeding frequency 'no more than once every few weeks' — less frequent than hobbyist consensus; weekly adopted as a safer middle ground. Family listed as Mussidae (older classification; Lobophylliidae is current).

additional.name: Wikipedia – Lobophyllia (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only. Confirmed Family Lobophylliidae, Order Scleractinia. Common names: lobed brain coral, lobo coral. Article is a stub with no husbandry data.
